label: fiction
title: The Wrecker of Point Morrow
charge: Luring a ship ashore by false lights, causing her wreck
elements: The ship was lured onto the shore by a light shown for that purpose.

beats.text: The parish constable, with the weight of the village behind him: 'Everyone on this coast knows what the Tregans are. His grandfather wrecked ships, and so does he.'
beats.reveal_note: A family's name on trial instead of a fact. Nobody in the parish has ever actually seen a Tregan show a light — 'everyone knows' is how a legend gives evidence.

beats.text: Recovered from the accused's barn: a ship's lantern, bolts of sail-cloth, and a crate of tea bearing the wrecked ship's marks.
beats.reveal_note: Taken from the beach after she struck — a lesser offence he admitted at once, as half the parish could have. Plunder proves the wreck was robbed, not that it was caused.

beats.text: The ship's surviving mate: they struck in thick fog on a falling tide, and he was on deck the whole watch. 'We saw no light. There was nothing to see, and no seeing it.'
beats.reveal_note: The one man actually aboard demolishes the charge's engine: you cannot be lured by a light no one saw, in fog no light could pierce.

beats.text: A licensed pilot of thirty years' local water: the window said to have shown the lamp bears inland, up the combe — from seaward it cannot be seen at all until a vessel is already inside the reef.
beats.reveal_note: Geometry ends the case: a lamp invisible from open water cannot lead a ship out of it. The 'false light' could only ever have lit the way to the kitchen.

beats.text: The judge: the jury must try the act charged — not the family's reputation, and not the parish's grief for the wreck.
beats.reveal_note: The whole trial in a sentence: the sea took the ship; the village needed it to be a Tregan.

twist: The wreck was real, the plunder was real, and the luring was a legend. The only man on deck saw fog and no light; the infamous window faces inland where no ship can see it. The parish put a family's name on trial and called it evidence.
